data.title: Q2 Holdings: Buy The Dip As Backlog Grows And Margins Expand
data.description:
Underneath the sharp stock market rally that has surprised investors this year, a trend has played out that gets little attention: the very top end of the S&P 500 has been driving all the gains (namely, large-cap tech stocks like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Oracle Corporation (ORCL)), while small and mid-cap stocks are getting sidelined. As I prepare my portfolio for 2026, I'm betting on a rotation the other way as investors seek a return to "growth at a reasonable price" stocks versus "growth at all costs."

Q2 Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:QTWO), in my view, is potentially a great rebound play to check off this box. The banking software company has been a major laggard this year, down ~30% year to date. The question for investors now is, can Q2 stage a comeback?

For me, timing is everything when it comes to investing in a choppy mid-cap growth stock like Q2 Holdings. I downgraded Q2 Holdings to neutral and recommended trimming the position in May, when the stock was trading close to $90. Since then, however, shares of Q2 Holdings have dropped a fresh ~25%, prompting me to take a fresh look at this name. Amid a recent beat-and-raise and strong secular tailwinds for greater automation in the perennially tech-lagging financial services sector, I'm upgrading Q2 back up to a "Buy."

For investors who are newer to Q2 Holdings, here is the long-term bull case for the company:

  • Q2 plays in a large and underpenetrated $23 billion TAM. Q2 Holdings is a "vertical" software company and one of the few software companies that specifically serve a very large niche: banks and financial institutions. The company’s current revenue profile, meanwhile, still represents a relatively low ~3% penetration into its overall market.
  • Land-and-expand business model. The company aims to be a one-stop shop that addresses the complete solutions and pain points relative to financial institutions, and it aims to cross-sell broadly within its customer base. Q2 notes that it has $3 billion of revenue growth potential from within its existing customer base.
  • Firm ARR scale. The company is built on top of nearly pure recurring revenue. Its ARR covers ~90% of its annual revenue guidance, giving the company plenty of revenue visibility and the capability to outperform its outlook.
  • Profit-centric. Despite its double-digit revenue growth, the company is already achieving adjusted EBITDA margins in the mid-20s percentage while also growing FCF at a >2x y/y clip.

Now, Q2 stock also has the benefit of a modest valuation on its side. At current share prices near $67, Q2 trades at a market cap of $4.19 billion. Netting off the $532.1 million of cash and investments against $493.4 million of convertible debt gives us an enterprise value of $4.15 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, Q2 has boosted its full-year revenue outlook to $783-$788 million (12-13% y/y growth), versus an 11-12% growth range previously. Meanwhile, for next year, FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting Q2 to generate $869.6 million in revenue, or 11% y/y growth. This positions the stock's valuation multiples at:

  • 5.3x EV/FY25 revenue.
  • 4.8x EV/FY26 revenue.

We can now stack up Q2 against other software companies that are currently growing at a low/mid-teens profile, such as Workday, Inc. (WDAY). The stock trades at a ~1-2 turn discount to such names:

In my view, it's a great time to buy the dip on this name.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Q2 Holdings' latest Q2 results in greater detail.

Revenue grew 13% y/y to $195.1 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $193.7 million (+12% y/y) by a one-point margin. We do note that revenue growth did decelerate two points versus 15% y/y growth in Q1, which is one of the core reasons that Q2's share price has been softening since then.

We do find hope, however, in the company's fierce RPO expansion. Total RPO soared 21% y/y to $2.4 billion, representing a sizable amount of contractual backlog that Q2 Holdings has yet to recognize as revenue. The company has attributed this growth to its success in upselling existing customers and driving expansion bookings.

We note that 54% of this RPO, or $1.3 billion, is set to be recognized within the next 24 months. Again, we note that Street revenue expectations for Q2 Holdings in FY26 are only ~$0.9 billion. If we stretch that across two years, Q2's current 24-month backlog already covers a significant portion of two years of revenue run rate, and it doesn't even include any new business that the company can bring in.

On the new sales front, the company notes that it's entering into the second half of FY25 with a strong pipeline. Per CEO Matthew Flake's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

We complemented that with success in the Tier 2 and 3 segments as well, which represented a meaningful portion of our bookings performance. We also had notable M&A activity amongst our customers in the quarter. We had 2 scenarios where an existing customer acquired another financial institution, each resulting in incremental bookings that carried the equivalent of a Tier 1 size deal [...]

As we look to the second half of the year, our pipeline remains solid and our outlook is positive. Even with our performance to date, we expect the majority of enterprise and Tier 1 activity for the year to land in the back half of 2025.

The company also continues to build on its profitability. As shown in the chart below, adjusted EBITDA grew 54% y/y to $45.8 million, which hit a 23.5% margin that improved 620bps y/y.

We note that at the current pace of profit expansion, Q2 may soon be able to justify its valuation from an adjusted EBITDA basis (applying a similar ~24% margin on FY26 revenue estimates yields a forward adjusted EBITDA multiple of only ~20x), which should provide support for its stock going forward. Q2's free cash flow in the first half of FY25 also grew 128% y/y to $79.5 million.

Key takeaways

Q2 is a solid software play with consistent growth results, a healthy backlog, and an expanding margin profile. My previous concern with Q2 was entirely around valuation, which has been more or less solved with the stock's ~30% slide over the past few months. Take advantage of this dip and view it as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Argan: Opportunities Remain, Investors Should Not Stay On The Sidelines
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Isaac Ch'ng as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Although Argan, Inc.'s (NYSE:AGX) stock price has risen by over 150% in the last 12 months, I believe significant upside potential still exists for investors to capture. As demand for energy sources grows, Argan’s ability to deliver both traditional natural gas-fired and renewable infrastructure is well-positioned to ride the tailwind, achieving sustainable backlog growth and revenue expansion, not just in the near term but also over the long term. Furthermore, the execution risk remains low due to its outstanding project record and consistent backlog burn rate. My three-stage FCFE valuation model also implies a difference between Argan’s current stock price (approximately $265 as of 30 September 2025) and its intrinsic value ($290 per share).

Thus, I’ll recommend a buy on Argan, Inc. stock, especially for investors who plan to have a 6- to 12-month investment horizon.

Business Overview

Argan is an energy infrastructure company that offers engineering, procurement, construction, project development, and maintenance services through its wholly owned subsidiaries. Argan has classified its business operations and revenue reporting into three major segments: power industry services, industrial construction services, and telecommunications infrastructure services.

Market Landscape: Backlog Momentum and Industry Outlook

Starting from the top line of the financial model, the reported robust project backlog has significantly influenced the operating income forecast for the next few quarters. Argan has recorded a $2 billion project backlog as of 31 July 2025 and is expected to surpass the $2 billion record by the end of this fiscal year. The backlog growth rate is supported by the current favorable macroeconomic context. Hyperscaler data center expansion, accelerating electric vehicle growth, and the broader electrification trend reinforced the sustainability of backlog growth. The United States Energy Information Administration has expected a spike in electricity consumption in 2025 and 2026, surpassing the highest record in 2024. All these statistics have proven the driving demand for reliable power resources.

Furthermore, if we dive into Argan’s project backlog details, we can see that the latest recorded project backlog has shown that Argan is mainly focusing on the construction of natural gas-fired power plants and renewable energy plants, representing 61% and 29% of the total backlog, respectively. The company assumes the backlog will continue to be driven by more natural gas-fired facility projects in the near or mid-term future.

Argan’s strategic focus on natural gas-fired projects is aligning well with the rising demand for natural gas in the current macro environment. In 2025, 2.6 GW of natural gas-fired capacity will be retired in the United States. A substantial portion of the US natural gas infrastructure is aging out. In addition, EIA also forecasted a high record of natural gas consumption (91.4 billion cubic feet per day) in 2025 and assumes an increase in natural gas consumption over most of the sectors.

The key question is whether Argan has the expertise and ability to offer construction solutions to meet this demand and execute projects. I feel it does.

Historical data have demonstrated Argan’s construction expertise and capacity to capitalize on this driving demand. Argan used to handle various sizes of power projects, with capacities up to 1.2 gigawatts. The construction of Guernsey Power Station in Ohio, the largest single-phase, natural gas-fired power plant in the United States, has further supported the words of its management, as Argan is capable of capturing value from this opportunity.

In addition, based on the historical quarterly backlog burn rate data, Argan has constantly delivered its backlog projects for the last 3 years and has shown a steady backlog burn rate. The consistent backlog burn rate and the stable liquidity and debt-free financial position underpin low execution risk despite the current large backlog. Besides that, the net project awards (net of cancellations) have remained positive and have trended upwards over the past 3 years, reinforcing the qualitative assessment.

Note that Argan has had a lower backlog burn rate for the past 2 quarters, but mainly due to the significantly higher backlog opening balance recorded compared to other quarters.

The above statements demonstrate Argan’s backlog quality, supporting my opinion that Argan can benefit from the sustainable energy demand trend, thereby reinforcing the bullish case. Since the backlog expanded, Argan has received full notice to proceed on several projects, changing from planning to the execution phase.

The issuance of the full notice to proceed indicates the start of mobilization payments and milestone-based revenue recognition. Large-scale awarded contracts such as the Trumbull Energy Center (950 MW), Gas Power Project (700 MW), and the Sandow Lakes Power Station (1200 MW) are already contributing to early-stage project activity and are expected to convert to revenue over the next 12 to 36 months, materially reduce the probability of revenue shortfall, and enhance the near-term revenue visibility.

Overall, both qualitative and quantitative assessments indicate that the sustainability and growth of the project backlog and revenue should continue to be a major drive for Argan’s intrinsic value, instead of a major concern for investors.

Operating Margin Trends and Concentration Risk

If we investigate Argan’s composition of revenues, 83% of consolidated revenue has come from the power industry services. The revenue from power industry services has significantly increased compared to the other two segments: industrial construction services and telecommunication infrastructure services. Investors might be concerned about the concentration risk.

However, Argan has achieved improving operating profit margin in the power industry services and makes me view the situation as an opportunity instead of a huge concern. The rising operating profit margin indicates better project execution for their main business segment, alleviating the concentration risk, strengthening the free cash flow generation, and supporting long-term valuation. It is reasonable to infer that Argan did make use of specialization and scale effects to create operational excellence.

A series of similar EPC contracts has been awarded, and Argan’s wholly owned subsidiary, Gemma, has executed multiple large combined-cycle projects (14 combined-cycle entries in Gemma’s project map). Two of Gemma’s recent awards, Sandow Lakes (1.2 GW) and Trumbull (950 MW), illustrate Argan’s repeatable EPC templates and vendor engagement (both projects used Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMEGF) as their turbine supplier) on a large combined-cycle build. All these projects provide the basis for reusable engineering templates and repeatable execution schedules. Furthermore, long-standing relationships with vendors, such as Siemens Energy, help shorten procurement lead times. I believe those scale and process advantages are visible in the improved margins and cash.

Larger projects also indicate that the fixed costs are spread over more megawatts of capacity; it is reasonable to assume the margins expand with Argan’s project backlog. Note that the forecasted operating margin I have used in my FCFE model is based on the historical average of the recent 4 quarters' operating margin, to better represent margins that have structurally improved in the last year. These project details, repeatable EPC execution, and improved operating margin reduce Argan’s concentration risk relative to a typical EPC profile.

In addition, Argan has relatively modest company-specific risks in the power industry compared to firms in the AI or semiconductor industry, as electricity and energy are essential and complementary to the broader electrification trend. The demand for energy infrastructure is stable and has a low-volatility replacement cycle, unlike products such as AI chips or fast-evolving tech products, which might be completely replaced by newer versions of products.

Given a strong and sustainable backlog profile, consistent execution, and an improving profitability margin, Argan’s operational metrics are used to form the basis for my FCFE-based intrinsic value valuation.

Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE) Model – Methodology and Assumptions

Building on these operational strengths, my FCFE analysis suggests that Argan's intrinsic value remains above its current market price, reinforcing the opportunity for investors to capitalize on the upside. I have forecasted FCFE over an explicit 4-quarter horizon (the next four quarters, deriving it from operating income adjusted for non-cash charges, minus maintenance and growth CapEx, +/- quarter-by-quarter working capital movements).

The forecasted FCFE growth rate for Argan for the next two years is considerably higher, supported by the robust and sustainable backlog amount and its strong financial position. The current project backlog is sufficient to cover the next two years of forecasted revenue even when there are no additional new contract awards (which is likely impossible). DuPont analysis has been further used to confirm that the high growth rate phase of 2 years is reasonable. Next, a transition period of 4 years would be assumed, and a 2.5% long-term growth rate will be used for the terminal value calculation.

For the terminal value forecast, I have used a conservative Gordon-growth approach (anchored to long-run GDP growth, 2.5%) and derived the discount rate from a CAPM-based cost of equity (assuming the cost of equity in the base case scenario will be 9.7%).

Valuation Verdict: Numbers That Matter

Based on my FCFE model, Argan is positioned to generate healthy equity cash flows over the next 4 quarters, underpinned by its quality backlog and debt-free balance sheet. In the base case, after adjusting for working-capital movements and recurring cash outflows, I estimate the FCFE per share should range from $270 to $313 per share (Base Case Scenario will be $290 per share), using a weighted average diluted share count of 14,131,000 on 31 July 2025.

With shares trading at approximately $265 (as of 30 September 2025), the outcomes reinforce my view that a Buy Recommendation is given, and the stock is to be monitored for a 6- to 12-month investment horizon.

FCFE Model – Supplementary Assumptions

In addition to the key operational metrics and assumptions that have been presented above, I will outline the other supplementary assumptions and calculations used in my FCFE valuation. The other key inputs include the non-cash adjustment, tax rate, fixed capital investment, and working capital investment.

The operating income has been used instead of the earnings before interest and tax to exclude the non-operating income. Furthermore, for the depreciation and amortization, I have excluded the amortization related to the solar tax credit investment from the forecast calculation, as it is a non-operating expense and located below the EBIT in the accounting treatment.

The fixed capital investment of Argan for the past 3 years has not been fairly stable, and no guidance is given in the Argan management discussions. I have proceeded to use the revenue-linked method (average of historical CapEx/Revenue ratio) to forecast the upcoming fixed capital investment.

Noted that, for the fixed capital investment, I have not included the investment in the solar energy project that is recorded in the financing cash flow into my forecast calculation. Argan has mentioned that the recorded solar energy projects are investments in limited liability companies. The purpose of these investments is to receive energy tax credits. These are considered equity investments and not core CapEx needed to sustain or expand operations.

Besides that, I am using the average of the effective tax rate in fiscal year 2025 and the 3 months ended 30 April 2025 as the estimation of the effective tax rate for the upcoming periods. Note that the tax rate reported in the 3 months ended 31 Jul 2024 is not included in my assumptions calculation. The effective tax rate for this period is significantly different from the historical effective tax rate or even the statutory federal tax rate (21%) because of the favorable tax benefit resulting from stock option exercises. This is treated as a one-off benefit and not sustainable.

Risks: Sweet Spot or Pressure Point?

Investors might be concerned about the concentration risk of Argan’s project backlog.

Argan’s current backlog is composed of 61% of natural gas-fired facilities, and expects the natural gas-fired facilities to be the main driver for their near-term to midterm revenue growth. Any incidents that cause delays or cancellations on future projects in Argan’s major business operations will significantly affect the revenue assumptions in my model.

However, historical data have shown a positive long-term trend for natural gas annual consumption in the United States, and it has accounted for 36% of the United States’ energy demand in 2023. The magnitude of its consumption makes it almost impossible to be replaced by an alternative energy source in the near future. Argan also focused on other renewable energy projects, such as solar projects, biofuel power plants, and water treatment plants, which helped to diversify the risks.

Recently, there were also statements of changes in beneficial ownership submitted by Argan, including one of its directors who sold a total of 16,557 shares of common stock on the open market within 4 days. Nonetheless, often directors may sell stocks for their own personal reasons and unrelated to the company’s prospects. The transaction size was modest and not material enough to indicate a bearish insider outlook.

Conclusion

The FCFE model outcome highlights Argan’s capability to consistently generate positive free cash flow and its high intrinsic value (Base Case Scenario will be $290 per share). The model assumptions and calculations are underpinned by the sustainable backlog momentum, consistent backlog burn rate, improving operating profit margin, and alignment with long-term energy demand trends.

Overall, Argan shows a favorable risk-reward profile, and holding Argan stock provides investors with exposure to the tailwinds of a sustainable energy-demanding macro environment. I would recommend a partial portfolio allocation to Argan with performance monitored over the next 6–12 months.

data.title: AngioDynamics: Discounted Sales Multiple With Real Growth Engines
data.description:
Thesis

Last time I looked at AngioDynamics, Inc. (NASDAQ:ANGO), I rated the stock a Buy. My reasoning was around the fact that the company has a growing set of medical technology products, especially AlphaVac, AngioVac, and NanoKnife, that could bring in more sales and help it grab a bigger share of the market. My main idea was that by shifting focus toward faster-growing areas of healthcare, they could see very strong long-term gains. That said, I also noted there are still some short-term challenges in how well the company can execute its plans, which definitely had an impact on its performance.

A one-time entry at that call, and the stock's been a dud, with today's gain added in, slipping around 5%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) outpaced it with a 14% gain.

After looking at the latest earnings report, I think AngioDynamics is undervalued compared to similar companies. Right now, the stock is trading at a big discount based on sales multiples, even though its MedTech division is growing at double-digit rates, profit margins are holding steady, and the company is on track to start generating positive cash flow.

Revenue Up, Margins Holding

AngioDynamics’ quarter comes down to three things: what they’re selling, how well they’re running it, and whether they’re keeping prices in line, and it all shows up in the profit and loss.

Total revenue went up 12.2%, reaching $75.7 million. Breaking it down, the Med Tech side grew a very welcome 26.1% to $35.3 million, while Med Device went up a scant 2.3% to $40.4 million. As for size and context, Med Tech now makes up 47% of all sales; that's a nice pop in growth compared to 41% last year, exactly the outcome they’ve been steering toward.

Auryon, that's AngioDynamics’ laser system for treating peripheral arterial disease (PAD), brought in $16.5 million, up 20.1%. For those keeping track, it marks the 17th quarter in a row of double-digit growth, with about $0.5 million coming from early international sales after last year’s CE Mark approval.

Meanwhile, mechanical thrombectomy brought in $11.3 million, up 41.2%, split between AngioVac at $8.0 million (up 37.1%) and AlphaVac at $3.3 million (up 52.3%). NanoKnife, the minimally invasive ablation system that uses a technology called irreversible electroporation, or IRE, totaled $6.4 million, up 26.7%, with disposable products leading at 31.3% growth as interest from urology grows ahead of the new Category I code starting January 1.

Profitability improved this quarter too. Gross margin edged up to 55.3%, which is 0.9 points higher than last year, even after a $1.7 million tariff expense that knocked off about 2.2 points. The margin gains came from selling more of the higher-profit Med Tech products like Auryon and AlphaVac raising prices in both Med Tech and Med Device.

Management said a lot of the margin improvements were pulled into the first half of the year, so we shouldn’t expect another big increase later on. While this is going on, tariffs and import regulations are still up in the air as a result of the investigation into imports that was conducted by the Commerce Department in late September. This may potentially delay cost relief and make it more difficult to prepare for global supply chains.

AngioDynamics: Costs, Cash, and Course Correction

The company kept costs under control, but you can really see it when looking below the gross margin. Operating expenses came in at $52.5 million, which is 69.4% of sales, an improvement from 74% of sales last year. Research and development (R&D) spending was basically flat at $6.4 million, or 8.5% of sales, and is moving closer to the company’s 10% goal as medical technology programs grow.

SG&A costs were $40.7 million, or 53.7% of sales, mainly because the company expanded its thrombectomy sales team and added commercialization support. Management increased coverage to 50 dedicated sales reps for AlphaVac and AngioVac, and they expect to grow the urology team the same way as NanoKnife procedures ramp up.

In terms of the earnings arc, that also improved. The adjusted net loss shrank to $4.2 million (that’s 10 cents per share) compared to $4.4 million (or 11 cents per share) last year. Adjusted EBITDA also turned positive at $2.2 million, after showing a small loss in the same period last year. As of August 31, the company had $38.8 million in cash, down from $55.9 million on May 31. One other strong point to keep in mind they still have zero debt and access to a credit line if needed.

Looking ahead, the company expects to have positive cash flow for the full year. They plan to use about $3 million in Q2, come in around breakeven in Q3, and then bring in strong cash flow in Q4, helped by higher sales, seasonal trends, and manufacturing improvements.

The company raised its outlook in the usual key areas. For fiscal 2026, they said that net sales are now expected to be between $308 million and $313 million, compared to the prior range of $305 million to $310 million. That would mean around 5% to 7% growth over last year’s $292.7 million. Meanwhile, the MedTech side’s expected to grow 14% to 16%, which is better than the earlier forecast of 12% to 15%. For the time being, the Med Device segment is expected to stay about flat, which lines up with the strategy of using that steady portfolio to fund Med Tech’s expansion.

Gross margin is still expected to land between 53.5% and 55.5%, even after accounting for a $4 to $6 million tariff impact this year. The outlook for adjusted EBITDA was raised to $6 to $10 million, up from the earlier $3 to $8 million.

Management wrapped up by saying they’ve got enough cash, growth is coming from thrombectomy and NanoKnife, and they’ll keep funding programs like AMBITION BTK. The plan is to make Med Tech profitable and keep it that way through fiscal 2026.

ANGO’s Valuation

For valuation, I’m looking at ANGO by comparing its sales-based valuation to other companies in health care. Right now, it’s trading much cheaper than its peers.

On a TTM basis, the company’s EV/Sales ratio is 1.40, compared to the health care sector average of 3.86; that’s about a 64% discount. Looking forward, its EV/Sales is 1.33 versus the sector’s 3.67, nearly the same gap.

While that’s happening, the TTM P/S is 1.55, compared to 3.78 for the sector. The forward P/S is 1.48, compared to 4.14. These numbers are also below ANGO's own five-year averages: 1.80 for EV/Sales and 1.86 for P/S, meaning they’re down about 17–18%.

My take on why ANGO's performed poorly since my last buy call (specifically in that time frame between January and March 2025 I highlighted above) is that investors had been extra cautious since the company's really not making much profit yet. So with profits too thin to matter, I think that the only real way to size it up right now is by sales, not earnings.

Right now, the setup looks a whole lot better for the stock.

Today’s post-earnings pop shows investors are starting to close the gap with peers if the shift in its business mix keeps paying off. Remember, management says Med Tech will grow double digits, margins will hold even with tariffs, and cash flow will turn positive for the year. Hard targets that could back a higher sales multiple.

Analysts seem to be on the same page here too. EPS is expected to fall hard in FY26 but then rebound with solid growth in FY 2027 and FY 2029. And when earnings turn up like that, valuations usually follow because Wall Street adjusts its forecasts higher.

Final Takeaway

I’m still calling AngioDynamics a Buy. The stock's trading at less than half the sales multiple of its competitors, while its medical technology business is growing at double-digit rates. Profit margins are holding up even with tariffs, and cash flow looks set to turn positive soon. At today’s price, the upside looks stronger than the downside. The key is making sure the company keeps up momentum in getting hospitals to use its thrombectomy products and continues to roll out NanoKnife with solid insurance reimbursement.

data.title: SOXL: The Trend Is Your Friend
data.description:
Back in January, when we had the big "DeepSeek moment," I gave a Buy rating to the Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:SOXL), with some necessary caveats. This fund offers investors leveraged exposure, aiming for 300% of the daily movement of the U.S. semiconductor index, which the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOXX) is based on, but without the leverage SOXL provides.

While I have been holding a very small position myself, I was reminded to take a look at it again when it hit a new high for the year.

SOXL is driven by big household chip names like Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Qualcomm (QCOM), and now Intel (INTC). These are the largest players in the space and, among others, make up the index that drives SOXL's leveraged movements.

It underwent significant volatility, as expected, and while at some point it was down over 50% from my recommendation, it is now up 50% from that point. I meant it when I gave those warnings about volatility; one thing I've found is that folks tend to overstate their risk tolerances, right up to the point where they're holding a loss of more than 30%.

Past that point, the leaves start to shake loose. We may have seen some of that in the April capitulation of SOXL, but those willing to hold on or even double down were rewarded.

Warning: Risk Ahead!

If that chart didn't scare some folks unsuitable for this instrument away, this might. Here are a few warnings that I gave with the article in January that are still incredibly relevant and critical in an investor's understanding of the risks imbedded in SOXL.

The fund itself is not really suitable for long-term investing due to its extremely volatile nature, but it can be used as a short-term trading vehicle to great effect.

After one decides to take on that amount of risk and build a position in a fund like SOXL, the next most important thing to consider is your entry point.

I must note that the risks here are gigantic, and the SEC notes:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs typically are designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that the performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

Investors should very carefully read that bulletin, as it contains more information about the risks that leveraged ETFs provide.

The fund's prospectus also includes a relevant warning that investors need to pay attention to and consider carefully:

The Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares (the “Fund”)seeks daily leveraged (3X) investment results and is very different from most other exchange-traded funds. As a result, the Fund may be riskier than alternatives that do not use leverage because the Fund’s objective is to magnify the daily performance of the NYSE Semiconductor Index (the "Index").The return for investors that invest for periods longer or shorter than a trading day should not be expected to be 300% of the performance of the Index for the period. The return of the Fund for a period longer than a trading day will be the result of each trading day’s compounded return over the period, which will very likely differ from 300% of the return of the Index for that period. Longer holding periods, higher volatility of the Index, and leverage increase the impact of compounding on an investor’s returns. During periods of higher Index volatility, the volatility of the Index may affect the Fund’s return as much as, or more than, the return of the Index.

The Fund is not suitable for all investors. The Fund is designed to be utilized only by knowledgeable investors who understand the potential consequences of seeking daily leveraged (3X) investment results, understand the risks associated with the use of leverage, and are willing to monitor their portfolios frequently. The Fund is not intended to be used by and is not appropriate for, investors who do not intend to actively monitor and manage their portfolios. For periods longer than a single day, the Fund will lose money if the Index’s performance is flat, and it is possible that the Fund will lose money even if the Index’s performance increases over a period longer than a single day. An investor could lose the full principal value of his/her investment within a single day if the Index loses more than 33% in one day.

Timing Is Key

As shown in the chart of the performance of my buy rating, assuming you held it the whole time — which is not advisable — this fund benefits most from the abilities of active investors to nail the timing; remember the folks who invested at the start of 2022, who ended up down 90% at some point, and then again in April 2025.

Of course, time the bottom of April 2025, and Bob's your uncle: 346% gain.

This timing piece is true for leveraged funds more broadly, but also semiconductors in particular. I highly recommend this article from my colleague Julia Ostian about the cyclical nature of the semiconductor business if you are interested in learning more about the fundamental cycles that semiconductors go through.

The AI Arms Race Continues

I see little reason for the current infrastructure buildout to slow, especially given the litany of deals and investment promises being made by corporations and nation-states to build out data centers in the U.S., chock-full of chips from the cast of chipmakers that make up SOXL's holdings.

While I believe that we are in a bubble regarding AI, I acknowledge the fact that having that knowledge doesn't make me any more likely to be able to position well. If it's really 1996 and not 2000, then it's still prudent to have more market exposure, right?

All that to say is that I think the best thing to do is stay alert but allow the flow of trillions of dollars of AI investments, which I covered in detail (with some great charts) in "AI is Becoming the Economy."

On Wall St., one of the many sayings traders toss at each other is "the trend is your friend," meaning that we shouldn't fight where things are going. This is one of the things that stops me from being a perma-bear about AI and the bubble we're in. The party could go on for a significant amount of time, and so there may still exist some good timing opportunities in SOXL.

I don't believe in my ability to call the bubble, to say "it's over," and then watch it pop. I may change my tune, but for now, the trend is my friend, and I am not fighting the meteoric rise in semiconductor stocks regardless of my instincts to call a bubble.

You can know you're in a bubble but have nothing to do about it other than prepare yourself for its eventual turn. That may be years away, so in the meantime, it's best to keep some stock with sectors that have been on a tear, like semis have.

SOXL Technical Analysis

One of the ways that I like to decide on entry or exit points is using technical analysis. Here's a quick chart glance and some takeaways that could help us navigate a SOXL trade.

Here is a one-month chart with hourly candles:

We see a breakout above previous resistance, a bullish signal. This is coupled with strong momentum, confirmed by the positive MACD and PPO indicators.

RSI is indicating oversold, at 76, but this level can be sustained for long periods of time with leveraged ETFs, so I do not give it too much weight here. Note that the first two weeks of September, which saw significant gains in SOXL, had the RSI pinned above 70 the whole time.

For tactical "dip-buyers," consider an entry point below $37.50, which would align with the support of the 20-day EMA (blue line), but also the first Fibonacci retracement level, a common support.

If SOXL falls below $35, which would likely also be a breakdown below the 50-day EMA (yellow line), I would expect more pain ahead. That could be a good sign to exit the position, especially if the RSI is still elevated and if momentum indicators roll over. They look like they might, especially given the volatility expansion we saw in the last few days with the widening Bollinger Bands (white lines).

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in this ETF. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I still see opportunity in SOXL with the AI party continuing to rage on, the semiconductor industry seemingly in an expansion phase of its cycle, and strong momentum in the technicals. However, risks remain high, and investors need to strongly consider not only the risk that high-valuation technology stocks bring on, but the risk of adding leverage to the mix as well.

Of course, with all good things, moderation is what can aid with long-term success trading these instruments. Make sure to position prudently and protect your downside, either with tools like options or by limiting your exposure overall.

I maintain my Buy rating on SOXL despite this past run-up on continued strength and momentum both technically and fundamentally. While we may be in a bubble in a larger macro sense, its unwinding is still likely a long time away, and current signs point to the exuberance continuing and thus higher valuations and returns for semiconductors. While that is still ongoing, I am still interested in semiconductor stocks and applying some leverage tactically.

Thanks for reading.

HNDL Strategy

Strategy Shares Nasdaq 7Handl™ Index ETF is a multi-asset fund of funds launched on 1/17/2018 tracking the Nasdaq 7HANDL™ Index. HNDL offers monthly distributions with a 12-month trailing yield of 6.74% and an expense ratio of 0.96%. As described in the prospectus by Strategy Shares ETFs:

The underlying index has a 50% allocation to fixed income and equity ETFs (the “Core Portfolio”) and a 50% allocation to ETFs of 12 asset categories (the “Explore Portfolio”). Additionally, the fund uses swap contracts on the underlying index to reach a 1.3 leverage factor.

The Core Portfolio is composed of 70% fixed income securities and 30% large-cap equities. The Explore Portfolio follows a momentum-based tactical allocation in high-yield asset categories, with a maximum of 9% in securities rated below investment grade (“junk bonds”).

The fund intends to maintain an annual distribution rate close to 7%. The portfolio turnover rate was 32% in the most recent fiscal year and 58% in the previous year.

data.title: Knife River: Even Though Shares Have Fallen, This Play Still Makes Sense
data.description:
A common saying in the stock market is that the time to buy is when blood is in the streets, even if that blood is your blood. This maxim certainly holds true in my view. But it does not mean that going through this process is easy or pleasant. It's never a great thing to endure declines in your portfolio, even if those declines are on a short-term basis. A good example of this can be seen by looking at Knife River Corporation (NYSE:KNF), a business that provides customers with crushed stone, sand, and gravel.

Back in March of this year, I decided to reaffirm the enterprise as a "Buy" candidate. This was based on the view that shares were attractively priced and that, in addition to growing in the past, the firm had multiple catalysts, including a nice acquisition that it had made. Since that time, however, the stock has plummeted by 20.8% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 16.3%. This is in response to a reduction in guidance from both a revenue and profit perspective. However, to my credit, I will say that the difference in performance between it and the market has not been as severe if we take it from the time I originally rated it a "Buy" in October 2023. Since then, the stock is up 48.5%, while the market is up slightly more at 55.1%.

To be honest with you, I believe there will be some weakness moving forward. Investors should be prepared for that. If you can't handle some volatility, cashing out and looking elsewhere may not be a bad idea. But for those with a long-term investment horizon, I would make the case that this prospect still has market-beating potential. Because of this, I believe that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is still the right choice.

Checking in with Knife River

As of this writing, the newest data that we have regarding Knife River covers through the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. The data that we have here is admittedly mixed, with most of the data points looking more negative than positive. On the positive side, revenue for the business came in at $833.8 million. That's an increase of 3.3% over the $806.9 million the company reported the previous year. According to management, the firm saw declines in two of the three sales categories that it has. For instance, aggregates declined from 9.41 million tons to 8.83 million tons, while asphalt declined from 1.81 million tons to 1.64 million tons. This brought sales of aggregates down from $158.4 million to $165.9 million, while asphalt revenue dropped from $119.3 million to $110.8 million.

According to management, demand has been declining in key areas. In Oregon, for instance, public funding for pavement under the state’s Department of Transportation has been budgeted at $100 million. That's down from the $140 million reported for 2024. And for 2026, funding is expected to fall even further to $80 million. This funding has been diverted from asphalt paving to larger projects that management classifies as mega projects. On the private side of things, there has been weakness as well. The number of construction cranes in Portland, for instance, has declined markedly because of economic uncertainty related to the trade war situation and other factors like existing projects experiencing delays.

Of course, the company doesn't just operate in Oregon. It has operations across the United States. This includes across the West Coast, across the Great Plains portions of the United States, and parts of the Midwest, Alaska, Hawaii, and Texas. The firm operates as one of the largest providers of aggregates in the nation. But this also means that issues that affect other regions of the nation also affect the business.

As an example, management mentioned in their latest quarterly report that parts of their Central and Mountain segments endured higher than normal rainfall that caused delays in work, impacting not only aggregate and asphalt revenues but also contracting services revenue. But the fact of the matter is that while prices have increased, volumes have come under pressure due to these factors. On the aggregate side of the business, for instance, total volume for the United States has been on the decline for the last couple of years now. This can be seen in the image above.

The good news is that some pain from declining volumes was offset by higher average selling prices. The price per ton for aggregates, for instance, grew from $16.84 in the second quarter of 2024 to $18.80 at the same time in 2025. But that's not all. The price for asphalt on a per-ton basis grew from $65.82 to $67.45. This overall trend is encouraging, and, as the chart above illustrates, pricing for these items, as well as for ready-mix concrete, has been rising over the last few years. Another thing that helped the company, speaking of ready-mix concrete, is the fact that overall volumes grew from 975,000 cubic yards to 1.04 million cubic yards.

Even though revenue for the company increased, profitability has taken a hit. Net income plummeted from $77.9 million to $50.6 million. Some of this was because of a rise in interest expense amounting to $8.4 million year over year because of a higher amount of debt on its books. In addition to this, the company saw construction materials costs grow from 71.3% of those sales to 76.4%. This seems to be in relation to lower production in the areas I mentioned, causing a deleverage effect in relation to the high fixed costs that the business has. When it comes down to certain services, such as those involving the energy side of the equation, the firm also experienced pain because of competitive market conditions and higher repairs and maintenance costs.

Other profitability metrics of the business largely worsened. The one exception was operating cash flow. It improved from negative $46.6 million to negative $42.5 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $109.8 million to $94.5 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business fell from $154.3 million to $140.8 million.

The same market conditions that negatively affected results during the second quarter of 2025 had a big role to play in impacting results for the first half of the year in its entirety on a year-over-year basis. In the chart above, you can see that revenue increased, but profits and cash flows worsened across the board. This is disconcerting to say the least and has pushed management to revise lower its guidance for the year. Previously, management anticipated revenue of between $3.25 billion and $3.45 billion. But that has now been pushed down to between $3.10 billion and $3.30 billion. Meanwhile, management has reduced guidance for EBITDA down to between $475 million and $525 million compared to the previous expected range of between $530 million and $580 million. And if we assume that the rest of this year will look like the first half of the year did on a year-over-year basis, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow to come in at around $213.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see what hitting these estimates would mean for the valuation of the company. I also included, for context, how the company is valued using results from 2024. Clearly, the picture looks less appealing now than it would if we used the 2024 results. However, relative to other similar firms, shares are still decently priced. In the table below, I stacked the firm up against five other similar enterprises. While on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, three of the five firms were cheaper than it is, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, only one of the companies was cheaper.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Knife River Corporation 20.1 11.2
Compass Minerals International, Inc. (CMP) 4.1 9.5
Eagle Materials Inc. (EXP) 13.7 11.6
Vulcan Materials Company (VMC) 24.0 19.6
United States Lime & Minerals, Inc. (USLM) 23.9 19.7
Martin Marietta Materials, Inc. (MLM) 19.6 19.8

Given the nature of the business, we should also pay some attention to leverage. This is especially true when we consider that the firm acquired another firm by the name of Strata Corporation in March of this year. This enterprise, which operated as a construction materials and contracting services provider that serviced North Dakota and parts of Minnesota, cost shareholders $454 million. But even with this purchase, the net leverage ratio of Knife River is not exactly high. In the chart below, I put, using the estimates for 2025, its net leverage ratio compared to the same five firms that I have stacked it up against. Three of the five are superior to it. But honestly, one of them is not terribly far off. In fact, to have the same net leverage ratio as the next closest below it, it would need to reduce net debt by only about $90.3 million. So while Knife River might not be the lowest leveraged player in the space, it's certainly not bad.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I continue to believe that Knife River remains an attractive prospect. Admittedly, current market conditions are proving to be painful for the business. And as time goes on, we could see some additional weakness on that front. This is especially true if I am correct about the direction the economy is heading. But in the long run, I think that this market is appealing, and the valuation of the business, while not exactly fantastic, is not awful given the circumstances. Because of this, I think that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is the right choice.

data.title: It's Netflix Vs. Elon Musk In Two Weeks (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Netflix, Inc. (NASDAQ:NFLX) in April, I downgraded to a Hold, as I believed that the stock may see significant volatility if the management revises their forward guidance lower from potential second-order headwinds from tariffs.

Since then, NFLX stock has performed more or less at par with the S&P 500 (SP500), although its stock price has declined over 12% since its all-time high it set in June.

In Netflix’s previous Q2 FY 2025 earnings report, the company beat both its revenue and earnings estimates, driven by more members, higher subscription pricing, and increased ad revenue.

Plus, during the quarter, Netflix also rolled out its Netflix Ads Suite, which is their in-house first-party ad tech platform, with early results that are in line with management’s expectations.

The company is scheduled to report its Q3 FY 2025 earnings on 10/21, and while management expects to see revenue accelerate higher in the quarter, Elon Musk, CEO of Tesla, has recently doubled down on “Cancel Netflix” calls on the X platform over ideological opposition to the streaming platform’s content.

Whether his campaign leads to more users cancelling their Netflix subscriptions is currently unknown. In this post, I have outlined what Netflix investors should be paying attention to in the Q3 FY 2025 earnings call.

In the meantime, I have upgraded my rating to a Buy with an updated price target of $1359 per share, as I remain optimistic about the buildout of its ad-supported tier with the rollout of Netflix Ad Suites, which will allow for better measurement, enhanced targeting, innovative ad formats, and expanded programmatic capabilities over time.

A Quick Summary of Netflix’s Q2 FY 2025 Earnings

Netflix reported its Q2 FY 2025 earnings in July, where both revenue and earnings beat estimates, with the former growing 16% YoY and operating margin expanding by 700 basis points to 34.15.

When it comes to revenue growth, the superior performance was driven by more members, higher subscription pricing, and increased ad revenue. Plus, its UCAN (United States and Canada) geography performed exceptionally well, with revenues accelerating from the previous quarter at 15% YoY and contributing roughly 44.5% of Total Revenue. In the meantime, Netflix’s other operating geographies also continued to perform well, growing at least in the high teens range, as can be seen below.

During the quarter, Netflix had a wide variety of hit series like Squid Game S3, Sirens, Ginny & Georgia S3, and more, as well as popular films like Tyler Perry’s STRAW and Exterritorial, with members watching over 96B hours on Netflix in the first half of the year, up 1% YoY.

The growth in user engagement is a bullish sign in my opinion, as this indicates that the company’s content strategy is resonating with its audience. Plus, in order to further deepen user engagement, Netflix is also developing shows and films that deeply connect with audiences in international markets by leveraging their creative teams and collaborating with local talent to produce stories that reflect their cultures and interests. This can be seen in the robust revenue growth rates across EMEA, LATAM, and APAC above, as their contribution to total revenue expands.

Aside from expanding its content slate to boost user engagement, Netflix also rolled out its new user interface that contains responsive recommendations, designed to help their members more easily find shows, movies, live events, and games they want to watch and play.

Turning our attention to user monetization, Netflix uses a two-pronged strategy, which consists of its ad-free and ad-supported tiers. During the quarter, the company continued to make progress on building their ads business by rolling out Netflix Ads Suite, which is their in-house first-party ad tech platform, with early results that are in line with management’s expectations.

Looking forward, Netflix management expects revenues to accelerate in Q3, with improving operating margin on a year-over-year basis. For the full year, Netflix has now raised their revenue forecast by 2.2% from its previous target to grow 15-16% YoY to $44.8-$45.2B, while targeting an operating margin of 29.5%, up 50 basis points from their previous target. Note that the management’s revenue guidance takes into account that ad revenue will double in 2025 and will continue to scale after that as its Netflix Ad Suites platform allows for better measurement, enhanced targeting, innovative ad formats, and expanded programmatic capabilities.

Elon Musk’s “Cancel Netflix” Campaign and Q3 Preview

Netflix is scheduled to report its Q3 earnings on 10/21 after hours, where consensus estimates point to revenue growing at 17.28%, higher than the 17% that management has guided for, along with a 28.74% growth in its earnings per share to $6.95 per share.

Meanwhile, when it comes to the full-year picture, the trend of upward revisions to both revenue and earnings has been positive on a 3M basis, with analysts expecting revenues to grow in the midpoint of management’s projections, as can be seen below.

Plus, what I will also point out is while the stock is down close to 13% since reaching an all-time high in June, it is still outperforming the S&P 500 YTD by over 2.2x, as it was perceived as a safe haven from the tariff turmoil at the start of the year.

Keeping that in mind, Elon Musk has recently doubled down on “Cancel Netflix” calls on X, citing ideological opposition to the streaming platform’s content. The controversy has shone a spotlight on ‘Dead End: Paranormal Park,’ the animated children’s series directed by Steele, which Musk claims is promoting ideological gender-related messaging to young viewers.

It is, however, unknown as to whether his calls in cancelling Netflix will create a short-term volatility in the number of paid members across the company’s ad- and non-ad-supported tiers.

While it should not affect the company’s Q3 results, investors will be paying attention to management commentary on its member addition and retention as well as watching as they expand their content slate across geographies while innovating on their core user interface and Netflix Ad Suites to drive user engagement and monetization.

Another metric to note will be user watch time. This will tell us whether Elon’s campaign has indeed managed to dent user engagement, although Netflix claims that their viewing is very broad and not dependent on any one title to drive engagement.

Finally, what will give the stock a much-needed boost is if management raises their full-year revenue guidance higher, especially as they continue to strengthen their new initiatives around live programming, games, and building out their Netflix Ad Suites ad platform to attract higher ad spend.

Revisiting My Valuation

Looking forward, I will take consensus estimates for revenue growth that see revenue growing 15.5% in FY 2025, followed by 13% and 10.9% YoY in FY 2026 and FY 2027, respectively, translating to $56.47B in total projected revenues by FY 2027.

From an earnings standpoint, earnings per share are projected to grow 32.75% YoY in FY 2025, followed by growth in the twenties range after that, resulting in $39.23 in earnings per share by FY 2027. This will be equivalent to a present value of $32.36 in earnings per share when discounted at 8%.

The stock is currently trading at 44x its FY 2025 earnings. However, when taking FY 2027 earnings per share into account, it is trading at 29x, which I believe is undervalued. Given the growth rate of Netflix's earnings over the next three years, I believe it deserves a premium of at least 2.5x that of the 10-year average S&P 500 price-to-earnings multiple.

This translates to a P/E ratio of 42, or a price target of $1359 per share, representing an upside of 16% from its current levels.

My Final Verdict and Conclusions

While the stock has outperformed the S&P 500 YTD, it is down over 12% since it created an all-time high in June.

At the moment, investors are looking for the next meaningful catalyst that will determine its future direction.

There is some noise at the moment as Musk doubles down on his “Cancel Netflix” campaign on the X platform. But whether it leads to a meaningful number of users dropping out remains to be seen.

In the meantime, the company has been expanding its content slate across geographies and improving its core user interface to boost user engagement while simultaneously showcasing progress on its Netflix Ads Suite platform to build out its long-term ads strategy.

data.title: URNM: Diversified Exposure To Leading Uranium Producers
data.description:
I wrote about the Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:URNM) on Seeking Alpha in late November 2024, where I concluded:

The trend is always your best friend in markets, and it remains higher in uranium and has turned higher in URNM after the September low. However, since risk is always a function of potential rewards, carefully consider risk-reward dynamics. While I favor a long position, approach the risk position with a clear plan when buying URNM shares at the current price level. I expect uranium prices to remain strong through 2025, which is bullish for uranium mining company shares and the URNM ETF product. Moving from the current $47.57 to the May high would result in an over 26% return, which I believe is a reasonable expectation for the ETF in the current environment.

URNM was trading at $47.57 per share on November 26, 2024. At nearly $60 per share in early October 2025, URNM was over 25% higher and near a new all-time high.

A bullish trend since April 2025

The Sprott Uranium Miners ETF has made higher lows and higher highs since reaching a significant bottom on April 7, 2025, the day the U.S. administration announced unprecedented tariffs on its global trading partners.

The daily chart highlights the over 125% explosive rally from the April 7, 2025, low of $27.60 to the most recent high of $62.12 per share on September 26, 2025.

URNM is a diversified approach to uranium miner investing

URNM’s top holdings include:

The ETF has a nearly 29% exposure to Cameco (CCJ) and the National Atomic Company Kazatomprom, the world’s two leading uranium-producing companies. URNM also has exposure to a portfolio of smaller producers, whose shares have experienced explosive rallies over the past five months.

A liquid ETF with a slightly above-market dividend yield

At around $60.00 per share, URNM had $2.18 billion in assets under management. URNM is a liquid ETF that trades an average of over 757,000 shares daily and charges a management fee of 0.75%. The annual $1.28 dividend translates to a 2.13% yield, which is above the SPY’s blended 1.09% yield. URNM’s dividend pays for the ETF’s expense ratio in under two quarters.

URNM’s fund profile states:

URNM is an effective tool providing exposure to the uranium market.

The long-term chart highlights the bullish trend in uranium prices (UXA:COM) since 2016.

Attractive Seeking Alpha ETF Grades and rankings

URMN’s Seeking Alpha's ETF Grades are:

The over 125% rally since April 7 earns URNM the highest possible A+ grade in momentum, and the assets under management and daily trading volume receive an A- in liquidity. Meanwhile, the 0.85% expense ratio receives a D+ in expenses, while the risk of highly volatile uranium prices gets a failing grade in the risk category. The D- in dividends is odd, as the ETF has a higher yield at over 2% than the SPY ETF.

URNM’s overall Quant rank is higher than 96% of other ETFs. In the sector asset class, URNM is ranked higher than 92% of other ETFs, and in the natural resources subclass, it is ranked higher than 89% of its peers.

The case for higher uranium prices and mining shares

The case for rising uranium prices and a continuation in the bullish trend for uranium mining company shares includes:

  • The trend in uranium prices has been higher since 2016.
  • The short-term trend in uranium mining shares has been explosive since the low of April 7, 2025. While corrections are likely, the bullish path of least resistance remains a compelling factor for the companies that extract uranium from the earth’s crust.
  • Uranium is the critical component in nuclear weaponry. The bifurcation of the world’s nuclear powers has increased military spending. NATO’s increasing contributions to its defense as a function of GDP support uranium demand.
  • There is a growing deficit in the uranium market as global demand for nuclear power increases. Utilities are seeking long-term supply contracts, spurring increases in new production.
  • Kazatomprom, the world’s leading uranium mining company, has revised its 2025 production guidance due to a shortage of sulfuric acid.
  • Bank of America has increased its 2025 uranium price forecast to $90-$100 per pound and expects the price to rise to $135 in 2026.
  • U.S. tariffs have created trade barriers that could continue to distort global prices, causing higher levels for the critical commodity.

URNM is a diversified ETF product that will continue to benefit from rising uranium prices. I rate URNM a Buy at the $60 per share level, as I expect the ETF to continue to reach new record highs over the coming months.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Yahoo's AOL Heads To Italy In Potential Sale To Bending Spoons
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Italy’s Bending Spoons is close to buying the company that once bought Time Warner. (0:15) Major music labels close in on AI deal. (1:04) Berkshire-Hathaway buys OxyChem. (1:34)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, “C’è posta per te!” Yahoo’s AOL may be heading to Italy.

Reuters reports that Apollo Global’s (NYSE:APO) Yahoo is in advanced talks to sell the legacy media brand to Italy’s Bending Spoons for about $1.4 billion. Sources caution no final deal has been signed yet, but talks are advanced.

Bending Spoons is known for snapping up struggling digital names and giving them new life. The Milan-based tech firm already has more than 300 million monthly users across its apps, and last month agreed to take video platform Vimeo private for $1.38 billion.

For Bending Spoons, AOL would bring a broad user base and fresh advertising reach. AOL has quietly seen traffic rise 20% year over year among younger users, thanks to new sections like Health, Fitness, True Crime and Science & Tech.

That $1.4 billion price tag would be 0.8% of what AOL paid to buy Time Warner in 2001.

And speaking of Warner, Warner Music (WMG) and Universal Music (OTCPK:UMGNF) are closing in on landmark AI deals.

The Financial Times reports that the labels could reach licensing agreements within weeks.

Startups such as ElevenLabs, Stability AI, Suno, Udio, and Klay Vision have been involved in talks, alongside larger players like Google and Spotify.

At the heart of the negotiations: how labels will license their songs for AI-generated tracks and training large language models. The labels want to get paid the same way they do from streaming services — with micropayments every time a song is used.

Among active stocks, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B) is buying Occidental Petroleum’s chemical business for $9.7B in cash. The move adds another non-insurance asset to Warren Buffett’s empire of fully owned companies.

Occidental (OXY) says it will use most of the proceeds — about $6.5B — to pay down debt, with the rest shoring up its balance sheet. The payoff: more than $350M a year in interest savings, plus stronger credit metrics and added financial flexibility.

Tesla (TSLA) delivered 497,100 vehicles in Q3 — a new quarterly record and well above Wall Street’s 448,000 estimate, as buyers rushed to lock in the expiring $7,500 EV tax credit.

Production came in at 447,000 vehicles. Of that, nearly all were Model Y and Model 3, with more than 481,000 of those delivered.

And FICO (FICO) is soaring after it announced a new licensing model that allows mortgage tri-merge resellers to calculate and distribute FICO Scores directly, eliminating reliance on the three nationwide credit bureaus. The program is expected to reduce average per-score fees by up to 50%.

Looking to the economy, there are more teething problems as Wall Street’s focus shifts to private data providers, with the shutdown halting official releases.

As first reported by Seeking Alpha, Challenger, Gray & Christmas’ September job cuts figures were released more than an hour before their scheduled time. Challenger said that was due to a scheduling error.

For the numbers, employers announced 54,064 job cuts in September, down 37% from August. But the year-to-date cuts are the highest since 2020.

On the hiring side, employers plan to add 204,939 jobs so far this year, down 58% from the same period in 2024 and the lowest year to date figure since 2009.

SA Analyst Damir Tokic says labor market numbers are signaling a recession, and this is consistent with other data.

Investors should “brace for recession and the recessionary bear market, as the current rally is clearly unsustainable,” he said.

In other news of note, Caterpillar (CAT) has suddenly become one of Wall Street’s surprise beneficiaries of the AI boom. Bloomberg reports that as soaring electricity demand ripples through the economy, investors are betting on Caterpillar’s lesser-known turbines.

The rally highlights how the AI trade has broadened. It started with chipmakers and cloud providers, then spread to utilities and data-center builders. Now investors are eyeing industrial companies that supply the power and infrastructure needed to keep AI running.

Caterpillar shares jumped 14% in September — their best month since 2023 — and are now up 32% on the year, handily outperforming the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ). The move gathered steam after Oracle (ORCL) forecast stronger cloud growth, fueling speculation that Caterpillar’s turbines will play a bigger role in meeting data-center energy demand.

Analysts say the story isn’t just about power. Oppenheimer’s Kristen Owen notes Caterpillar also sells the mining equipment needed to dig up copper and the machinery used in constructing data centers. Lower interest rates could revive traditional building activity too, giving the company multiple growth drivers.

And Bank of America recently lifted its price target to $517, calling Caterpillar’s power-generation unit its highest-margin business.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, how about 12 top takeout targets in tech?

Wedbush Securities says deal activity is likely to accelerate as AI spending continues on a tear. And smaller firms will become prime takeover targets.

Wedbush notes that while AI keeps racing ahead with new features and large-language model updates, many startups don’t have the cash to keep up with soaring costs for engineers and computing power. That makes them ripe for acquisition.

Among the potential candidates:

Tenable (TENB), a cybersecurity firm.

Qualys (QLYS), another security software player.

SentinelOne (S), known for AI-driven endpoint protection.

Elastic (ESTC), with its search and analytics platform.

C3.ai (AI), one of the most recognized pure-play AI names.

And TripAdvisor (TRIP), which could have strategic value in an AI-driven consumer environment.

data.title: Curaleaf Holdings Gains On Renewed Hope Of Cannabis Rescheduling In The U.S.
data.description:
Curaleaf Holdings Overview

Curaleaf Holdings, Inc. (OTCPK:CURLF) (TSX:CURA:CA) reported in line financial results for Q2 2025 last August. The company announced that it will begin the expansion of international operations into Turkey. The EU recently announced new regulations to allow its member states to legalize medical and recreational cannabis. Curaleaf Holdings foresees the European market ultimately rivaling the US market.

Meanwhile, back in the US, Trump posted a video to Truth Social about the benefits of CBD, which sparked a new sector rally on the hope of cannabis rescheduling. This rally originally began back in August when Trump first announced the administration’s intent to consider rescheduling.

The company’s stock price is up 230% over the last six months but down 1% over the last year. It has increased 195% since I covered it on Seeking Alpha last July. Domestically and globally, Curaleaf Holdings has a lot to gain with these new developments. I believe the renewed rally will likely subside and I continue my rating of a Hold for the company.

Operations and Synergies

Curaleaf Holdings, a US MSO or multi-state operator, grows, manufactures, distributes, and sells cannabis products. It operates in 17 US states and 15 countries. In the US, the company has 154 cannabis retail shops. Florida represents its largest footprint with 67 retail locations. The company operates wholesale operations domestically and internationally. It has 15 cultivation sites. Curaleaf Holdings has six brands: Grassroots, Find Flower, Anthem, Reef, The Hemp Company, and Select.

Curaleaf Holdings current domestic operations per state are as follows: Maine (4 retail shops), Massachusetts (4 retail shops), Connecticut (4 retail shops), New York (6 retail shops), New Jersey (3 retail shops), Pennsylvania (18 retail shops), Maryland (4 retail shops), Florida (67 retail shops), Ohio (2 retail shops), Kentucky (processing facilities), Illinois (10 retail shops), Missouri (processing facilities), North Dakota (4 retail shops), Arizona (16 retail shops), Utah (4 retail shops), Nevada (6 retail shops), and Oregon (licensed brand).

The company’s international operations include the UK (processing, manufacture, distribution, clinics), Germany (wholesale), Sweden (wholesale, clinics), Poland (wholesale), France (clinical testing), Switzerland (wholesale), Italy (wholesale), Spain (processing, extraction, manufacturing), Portugal (post-harvest facility), Australia (wholesale), New Zealand (wholesale), and Canada (indoor grow facility, wholesale). Curaleaf Holdings has received a license to sell cannabis in Turkey and will begin sales there in 2026.

Curaleaf Holdings also sells hemp-derived THC products, including drinks and gummies. The company has an e-commerce site and it sells through local distribution partnerships. The company has partnered with DoorDash in 15 states and it has its products in over 100 Total Wine & More shops across 12 states. In Florida, the company operates a hemp-derived THC retail location.

There have been some interesting developments for the company. Its entry into Turkish cannabis markets is top of the list. The company also received approval from the EU for its liquid cannabis inhalation device, which it has already released in the U.K. Curaleaf Holdings will release this product across its European and Australian markets. Curaleaf Holdings recently bought out its minority partners in Curaleaf International.

In the US, the company has launched its Anthem pre-rolls in six states, including New York, New Jersey, Florida, Arizona, Massachusetts, and Illinois. Anthem also released an infused pre-roll called Anthem Bold, which is available in New York, New Jersey, Illinois, and Arizona. The company’s new extraction technology called ACE is now being used in products released in New York. ACE extraction promises a cleaner and more potent concentrate.

Curaleaf Q2 2025 Results

Curaleaf reported Q2 2025 revenues of $315 million, representing a 1.5% increase QoQ and an 8% decrease YoY. Domestic sales were flat due to competition and price compression. International sales increased to $41 million, representing a 17% increase QoQ and a 62% increase YoY. The company reported increased demand in Germany and the U.K. The company reported gross profit of $152.6 million and gross margins of 49%.

Net loss was $53.2 million compared to a net loss of $61.1 million QoQ and a net loss of $48.9 million YoY. The company generated $9 million in operating cash flow. It reported $102 million in cash and ST investments. Curaleaf paid $47 million in debt and interest payments during Q2. Total debt is currently $560 million.

Market consensus for the company’s Q3 2025 revenue is $317.17 million, representing an increase QoQ and a decrease YoY. The company reports on November 11th. Curaleaf Holdings did not share its outlook for Q4.

The company’s stock price is currently overvalued according to its book value per share and NAV value per share. It is undervalued according to its forward multipliers like NTM total EV / Revenues. Enterprise value is higher than its market cap, which also implies undervaluation. The company’s expansion domestically and internationally warrants a higher stock price and valuation. Uncertainty in the cannabis sector keeps the overall rating at a Hold.

Curaleaf Holdings has the highest revenues and second-highest total assets compared to its peers. It reports the highest net loss. The company is outperforming its peers in terms of domestic and international footprint. Trulieve has the most retail dispensaries, while Curaleaf holds 3rd place. Curaleaf Holdings is undervalued compared to its peers and its future potential. The US MSOs have a lot to gain from cannabis rescheduling.

Risk and Investment Strategy

Curaleaf Holdings is at low risk of poor financial performance. The company’s assets outweigh its liabilities. Its debt is proportionate, and it has plenty of liquidity. The company’s current strategy of domestic and international expansion is in full swing. Competition and price compressions in its domestic markets continue to negatively impact revenues.

A Hold rating for the company comes at a low to moderate risk. The price has recently rallied, but it has not returned to its previous highs from last year. The price may drop as the current rally fizzles out over uncertainty. Investor sentiment is improving in the sector. In the long term, the company is well-positioned for growth.

Conclusion

US MSOs and Canadian cannabis LPs rallied this last week on renewed hope of cannabis rescheduling. The rally has subsided for now and uncertainty remains as to when the administration will reschedule cannabis. Curaleaf Holdings recently reported in-line financial performance for Q2 2025. The company’s international footprint is quickly expanding with the entry into Turkey and its new cannabis inhalant approved by the EU. Domestic growth is stagnant for most of the US cannabis companies. Curaleaf is ahead of its peers in performance. I continue my current rating of a Hold for the company.
